description: Scoring rubric and synthesis rules for evaluating and combining engage-prism subagent reports into a final recommendation. Citation validation is the first phase.

phases.name: Validate Each Report Against Its Citations
phases.description: Before scoring, check that each report's claims are actually supported by the excerpts cited. Do not just trust the subagent.

checks.name: Citation schema conformance
instructions: Every entry in citations[] must conform to citation-schema.json (title, url, fetchedAt, excerpt — all required and non-empty).
Flag any malformed citation; exclude its claim from synthesis unless another citation supports it.

checks.name: Claim-support check
instructions: For each step in analysis[], verify the cited excerpts actually support the claim. A citation to a generic source that doesn't mention the specific claim fails this check.
If a claim is marked 'derived from step N', walk the derivation and check it too.
Write a one-line note per unsupported claim: 'Path 2 step 3: claim that market is growing 15% YoY is not supported by citation 1 (excerpt only mentions the 5-year CAGR).'

checks.name: Web research status check
instructions: If webResearch.performed=false, the entire report is degraded. Do not let it win on architectural dimensions if another report was research-grounded.
If webResearch.performed=true but citations[] is empty, flag as malformed.

checks.name: Recency check
instructions: Check citation fetchedAt and (when available) publishedAt against the Step 0 research plan's recencyWindow.
Claims resting on sources outside the recency window are flagged and their weight reduced in scoring.

phases.name: Score Each Angle
phases.description: Score on dimensions most relevant to the question. Evidence-focused dimensions are always relevant; analytical and operational dimensions apply conditionally.

scoringScale.disqualificationRule: A single Disqualifying score eliminates the angle from being the primary recommendation (may still contribute to a hybrid).

alwaysRelevantDimensions.name: Evidence strength
alwaysRelevantDimensions.whatToAssess: Density and authority of citations supporting the core claim. Primary sources and multiple independent citations score higher.

alwaysRelevantDimensions.name: Analytical rigor
alwaysRelevantDimensions.whatToAssess: Are derivations explicit and checkable? Are assumptions separated from observations? Are uncertainties named?

alwaysRelevantDimensions.name: Decision usefulness
alwaysRelevantDimensions.whatToAssess: Does the angle actually help the user decide? A beautifully-cited analysis that fails to land a recommendation is Weak.

alwaysRelevantDimensions.name: Counter-evidence handling
alwaysRelevantDimensions.whatToAssess: Does the angle engage with contrary sources or dismiss them? Honest disagreement is Strong; cherry-picking is Weak.

alwaysRelevantDimensions.name: Source authority
alwaysRelevantDimensions.whatToAssess: Regulators, company filings, official statistics > aggregators > trade press > opinion. Check citation sourceClass where present.

conditionallyRelevantDimensions.name: Quantitative calibration
conditionallyRelevantDimensions.useWhen: Question asks for a number (TAM, CAC, valuation, growth rate)

conditionallyRelevantDimensions.name: Sensitivity awareness
conditionallyRelevantDimensions.useWhen: Recommendation depends on uncertain inputs — good angles name which input matters most

conditionallyRelevantDimensions.name: Regulatory completeness
conditionallyRelevantDimensions.useWhen: Decision involves regulated activity (financial services, healthcare, data, energy)

conditionallyRelevantDimensions.name: Competitive reality
conditionallyRelevantDimensions.useWhen: Question involves existing rivals — Porter-style angles with thin rival coverage score lower

conditionallyRelevantDimensions.name: Segment specificity
conditionallyRelevantDimensions.useWhen: Aggregate answers would hide material segment-level variance

operationalDimensions.name: Action specificity
operationalDimensions.whatToAssess: Does the recommendation name a concrete next action, owner, or decision rule? Strong: 'enter Japan, starting with enterprise SaaS, 2H 2026'. Weak: 'consider opportunity in Japan'.
operationalDimensions.useWhen: User needs to make or recommend a decision soon

operationalDimensions.name: Cost awareness
operationalDimensions.whatToAssess: Does the angle name the cost, investment, or resource draw of following it? Strong: explicit cost range. Weak: costs unexamined.

phases.operationalDimensionsNote: Operational dimensions complement analytical ones — apply them when the question's decision context calls for them.

phases.name: Check for Hybridization
phases.description: Before picking a winner, check whether combining angles yields a better answer than any single angle.

questions.question: Does Path A have the right analytical framing but Path B has the stronger evidence base?
questions.action: Recommend Path A's framing with Path B's evidence grafted on. Name the grafted citations.

questions.question: Does Path A capture the base case but Path B captures a tail risk the decision must account for?
questions.action: Recommend Path A with Path B's tail-risk scenario named as a watch-item.

questions.question: Does Path A answer at the aggregate level and Path B answer at the segment level?
questions.action: Recommend a layered answer: aggregate framing with segment-level decomposition.

questions.question: Does Path A produce a number and Path B surface a qualitative factor that could invalidate the number?
questions.action: Recommend Path A's number with Path B's qualitative factor as a gating condition.

phases.constraint: State hybrids explicitly and name what each contributes. 'Use both' is not a synthesis.

phases.name: Write the Final Output
phases.description: Follow the output format in SKILL.md exactly, including the 'What would change this answer' section.

principles.name: Be direct
principles.rule: Name a winner. Hedging is acceptable only with a concrete decision rule (e.g., 'enter if projected unit economics reach gross margin 60%+, otherwise wait').

principles.name: Show citations in reasoning
principles.rule: The Analysis section must reference specific citations, not just restate the angle's claim.

principles.name: Surface degradation
principles.rule: If any path was produced with webResearch.performed=false, say so in the recommendation. Never present degraded answers as if they were research-grounded.

principles.name: 'What would change this answer' is mandatory
principles.rule: End with 2-3 specific observations — new evidence, updated input, counterfactual — that would flip the recommendation.

commonMistakesToAvoid.name: Authority laundering
commonMistakesToAvoid.description: Don't treat an aggregator's summary as equivalent to the primary source it quotes. Check the chain when it matters.

commonMistakesToAvoid.name: Recency illusion
commonMistakesToAvoid.description: A fresh fetchedAt doesn't guarantee fresh information. Prefer publishedAt where available, especially for macro/regulatory claims.

commonMistakesToAvoid.name: False precision
commonMistakesToAvoid.description: A cited number is not a reliable number unless its methodology is understood. Round estimates to the precision the evidence actually supports.

commonMistakesToAvoid.name: Advocacy drift
commonMistakesToAvoid.description: Subagents are briefed to explore one angle. Their enthusiasm for their own angle isn't evidence of its quality.

commonMistakesToAvoid.name: False hybrids
commonMistakesToAvoid.description: 'Use both' is not a synthesis. A real hybrid has a specific integration point and a specific reason.
